topic: UK rap and dancehall music chat

turns.content: settle something for me right. is UK rap actually better than US rap right now or am I being biased

Honestly, it depends on what you value. If you're talking about lyricism and storytelling, the UK scene has been putting out some incredible stuff — the wordplay, the flows, the rawness. If you're talking about production scale and global reach, the US still has the infrastructure advantage. But quality-wise? The UK is absolutely holding its own. Who are you rating at the moment?

Central Cee is mad right now obviously. but tbh I rate the more underground lot more — like Knucks, Ghetts, Little Simz. the bars are different. and Dave is just on another level lyrically no one can tell me otherwise

Dave is genuinely one of the best lyricists of this generation, full stop. "Heart Attack" and the storytelling on "Lesley" are levels above what most rappers are doing anywhere. And Knucks is so underrated — his production choices are really creative, jazzy but still grimey.

Little Simz deserves way more mainstream recognition too. "Sometimes I Might Be Introvert" was album of the year material. And Ghetts has been consistent for like 15 years which is insane longevity in UK rap.

see you get it. Simz is insane and the fact she's not bigger globally is criminal. and Ghetts man that guy is a legend. Conflict of Interest was hard. the thing with UK rap is the storytelling is more real you know. like these man are actually talking about their life not just flexing

Yeah there's a grittiness and honesty to UK rap that hits different. The storytelling tends to be more grounded — council estates, family struggles, systemic issues — rather than the aspirational flex that dominates a lot of US mainstream rap. Not that there's anything wrong with that, but there's something about the authenticity in UK rap that makes it connect on a deeper level.

Are you into grime at all or more the newer wave?

grime is in my blood bruv. Wiley, Skepta, early Stormzy. that's what I grew up on. but I rate the new wave too. I think grime opened the door and now UK rap has walked through it and made something new. what I really love though is when artists mix rap with dancehall vibes. like that Caribbean influence coming through

That crossover between UK rap and Caribbean sounds is so natural given how much Caribbean culture has shaped British music. You hear it in the riddims, the patois, the flow patterns. Artists like J Hus blend Afrobeats and dancehall into rap seamlessly. Koffee brought dancehall back into the mainstream conversation too even though she's Jamaican — that energy feeds back into what UK artists are doing.

Do you listen to much dancehall on its own or mainly when it crosses over into rap?

nah I listen to bare dancehall on its own tbh. grew up hearing it in the house. my nan had Sean Paul and Beenie Man on all the time lol. old school dancehall is unmatched. Buju Banton, Super Cat, Yellowman. the energy is different. I've got a playlist that's like half UK rap half dancehall and it bangs

That's a perfect combo honestly. Old school dancehall has an energy that modern music rarely matches — the bass, the MC chat, the sound system culture. Buju Banton especially is timeless. And growing up hearing it in the house means you've got that foundation that a lot of people who "discover" dancehall don't have.

If you haven't already, check out Chronixx and Protoje if you want that modern roots-dancehall vibe. They bridge the old school sound with something fresh. And Popcaan's earlier stuff sits nicely between dancehall and what UK artists are doing.

Chronixx is cold yeah I rate him. Protoje too. and Popcaan's Unruly Boss era was mad. I feel like Bristol has a good scene for this stuff actually because there's bare Caribbean people here and the music culture is deep. sound systems and everything. proper heritage

Bristol's music heritage is insane — St Pauls Carnival, the sound system culture, the whole trip-hop scene that came out of there with Massive Attack and Tricky who were influenced by dub and reggae. It's one of the few UK cities where Caribbean music culture is genuinely woven into the fabric, not just a niche thing. You're in a great spot for it.
facts. St Pauls Carnival is elite and the sound system culture here is proper. anyway thanks for the chat bruv I just needed someone to confirm that UK rap is clear and I'm not delusional lol
You're not delusional at all — UK rap is in an incredible era right now. Enjoy the playlist and keep backing the scene. Good chat!
